seo.title: NiceChunk Player Sessions: Temporary Authority Without a Key Sandbox
seo.description: Learn how one wallet approval creates an expiring NiceChunk PlayerSession, what the ordinary session key can still sign, which actions consume the pass, and what refresh, funding, logout, expiry, and Guardian do not protect.

hero.title: One Wallet Approval Can Create an Expiring NiceChunk Pass—but It Does Not Sandbox the Key
hero.summary: The repository's production /play/ source route is /play/index.html → /play/main.js → play/*, where play-chain-adapter.js resolves a chain module. A retained 2026-07-19 UTC Nginx snapshot instead found /play/index.html referencing a versioned runtime bundle; those runtime bytes name a versioned chain asset as the default candidate when no global or localStorage override wins, and the audit fetched and hashed that candidate separately. The actual module a browser resolves—not an unbuilt source file—defines live behavior. A wallet approval can create an expiring PlayerSession record around a browser-held Keypair, but the Keypair and its SOL do not expire with the record, and the pass does not sandbox it.

visual.alt: NiceChunk coastal authorization waystation where the canonical black-haired villager boy uses a durable owner seal beside a separate copper session key while the canonical orange-haired villager girl checks an hourglass and blank stone ledger.
visual.caption: The owner seal, ordinary copper key, hourglass, blank record, and open path toward mining, building, and storage introduce a long-lived owner and an expiring gameplay pass while keeping the key visibly ordinary. This generated scene uses the canonical villagers, real Chunk.js coast, and baked game materials; it does not perform wallet approval, create a PlayerSession, prove expiry, sandbox a signer, or show current chain state.

guideContext.question: If one wallet approval lets the game keep signing for me, exactly what is authorized, where is the secret stored, what can spend the session-authority address's SOL, and how do I really stop it?

items.value: This was a fixed-public-file static HTTPS GET and byte-hash audit, not a browser HAR or instrumented browser session. The production snapshot is a point-in-time observation, not a permanent or future guarantee. It did not invoke placement or Backpack functions, observe a browser dynamic import, or read, set, or exercise any player's global or localStorage override; the returned code bytes only reveal that such override capability exists

items.label: Served feature-byte boundary
items.value: The fetched versioned default chain candidate bytes contain the literal function body result chain-placement-disabled, but the static snapshot did not invoke the placement function. Those served chain bytes also contain unified Game Program Backpack namespace one plus both discardBackpackResourceAt and discardBackpackResourcesAt exports; for this static Backpack evidence, no instruction or transaction was constructed or sent. The default fetched candidates lacked the recordBulkMineOnChain export

items.label: Runtime trust boundary
items.value: The adapter can prefer a global override or localStorage nicechunk.chainModuleUrl before its versioned and manifest candidates, so those two override mechanisms can change the active module bytes. Dynamic import evaluates module code before later export-shape validation, so never set an untrusted URL; this is a capability warning, not evidence of an observed exploit or compromise

terms.meaning: The long-lived player public key tied to the PlayerProfile. Think of it as the identity named on the house deed, not the browser's separate session-purpose working key.
The public key presented as the signer for a session-aware action. In plugin-wallet mode it belongs to a separate browser-held Keypair; in Local Game Wallet mode it is the owner key itself.
A public address plus private signing bytes. Whoever obtains the private bytes can produce valid signatures for that address; a PlayerSession does not rewrite what the key can sign.
A 184-byte public account owned by the NiceChunk Player Program. Its authorization record can expire; the separate Keypair, address, and SOL do not expire or disappear with it.
A deterministic account address derived from seeds and a program ID. The PlayerSession address uses the word session, the owner public key, and the session-authority public key.

An account marked is_signer for the current instruction. In the reviewed direct browser setup this normally comes from a transaction signature by each distinct key; a program-derived signer in a CPI can instead be authorized with invoke_signed. One matching public key can satisfy both direct setup roles.
One yes-or-no position inside allowedActions. Bit index 1 has value 2 and is named BREAK_BLOCK; bit index 2 has value 4 and is named PLACE_BLOCK.

A downstream contract handler branch that reads PlayerSession before performing its own action. The audited capability surface includes Chunk, Building, and Backpack code; that does not prove one default Play asset exposes every branch.
A boundary that would technically prevent a key from signing anything outside an approved set. Current PlayerSession is not such a boundary; it only governs programs that choose to read it.
Origin-local data available to browser code. The plugin-session secret is stored as Base64 under an owner-scoped key, which is convenient encoding rather than encryption.
Updating the allowedActions, expiresAt, updatedSlot, maxActions, and resetting actionCount on the same PlayerSession PDA. It is not a revoke operation.
Solana's native program for ordinary SOL transfers and account operations. A normal Keypair can sign a System Program transfer without involving NiceChunk PlayerSession.
A persistent browser wallet whose Base58 private key is stored separately. Current session setup uses its same long-lived key as both owner and sessionAuthority.
The Clock sysvar timestamp read inside a program. Consumer expiry is decided against this chain timestamp, not merely the browser's clock.
A position in a packed Backpack array. Removing one position shifts every later record left, so indexes describe current positions rather than permanent item identities.
NiceChunk's separate realtime and region system. Its current contract, HELLO message, and runtime client do not issue, validate, refresh, or revoke PlayerSession.
A saved read-only observation from one RPC context. It can prove what was returned at that moment, not a permanent network population, Mainnet state, or possession of any secret key.
Lamports are the smallest SOL units: 1 SOL equals 1,000,000,000 lamports. The browser's 0.1 SOL policy is 100,000,000 lamports.

Lamports held by a data account so it can remain allocated. PlayerSession PDA rent is separate from spendable SOL at the session-authority address, and the reviewed program has no Session close refund.

A bundle is packaged browser code; a module is one JavaScript unit; the loaded asset is the exact response chosen at runtime. Repository source can explain intent but cannot prove which packaged bytes a live browser resolved.
An origin is the site's scheme, host, and port storage boundary. A prefix is the beginning of a localStorage key; logout scans several prefixes without restricting the wallet suffix.
RPC is the read interface to a Solana node. A slot identifies a ledger position. Confirmed is a commitment level with strong cluster support but is not the same word as finalized.
Devnet is Solana's public test network; Mainnet is the production-value network. A Devnet account snapshot must never be presented as Mainnet population or balance evidence.
PlayerProfile is the player's public identity account; GlobalConfig names shared game configuration and world context. Session setup and consumers compare selected relationships to these accounts.
A reversible text encoding used for the Local Game Wallet secret. Like Base64, it changes representation rather than encrypting or protecting the private bytes.

keyPoints.title: The pass limits a NiceChunk consumer, not the Keypair
keyPoints.body: A consumer can reject a session-aware request after expiresAt or when its action bit is missing. The same ordinary session Keypair can still sign an independent System Program transfer that never references PlayerSession or invokes the Player Program.

keyPoints.title: Setup has two signer roles, even when one key fills both
keyPoints.body: Create or refresh requires owner and sessionAuthority to be signers. Distinct plugin-wallet keys produce two signatures; Local Game Wallet puts the same public key in both roles, so one signature satisfies both checks.

keyPoints.title: The chain record is public; the signing secret stays client-side
keyPoints.body: The 184-byte account exposes public keys, mask, expiry, and counters. Plugin mode saves the private session bytes as Base64 in owner-scoped localStorage; Base64 is not encryption and never belongs in the public record.

keyPoints.title: BREAK_BLOCK currently reaches much farther than its name suggests
keyPoints.body: Fifteen audited contract Session-aware branches use bit index 1, value 2: five Chunk handlers, eight Building tags 0–7, and two four-account Backpack branches. That capability count is not proof that one default Play asset reaches all 15; the retained live asset confirms unified Game Backpack routing and disabled placement but exposes no bulk-mining export.

keyPoints.title: 10,000 is stored, not enforced
keyPoints.body: Repository Session builders request PlayerSession read-only, handlers only borrow and inspect it, no consumer calls Player Program to increment it, and no handler compares actionCount with maxActions. Those independent facts—not the meta alone—make 10,000 a stored value rather than a current action limit.

keyPoints.title: Refresh, logout, and Guardian are not revocation
keyPoints.body: A same-key request selects the same PDA but rewrites it only when all stored relationships and current Profile checks pass; a new key creates another rent-funded PDA without stopping or refunding the old one. Logout only attempts best-effort local deletion and changes no chain account or SOL balance, while Guardian remains independent.

keyPoints.title: Chain setup and browser secret saving are two separate commits
keyPoints.body: The owner transaction may broadcast or confirm before the new session secret reaches localStorage. A storage failure, ambiguous confirmation, or page close can leave funded authority and PDA state without a durable browser key; retries can create and fund additional authorities.

sections.title: One player may use two keys, but the working key remains an ordinary Solana signer
paragraphs: Start with one player and two account roles. The owner is the long-lived identity behind the PlayerProfile. The sessionAuthority is the key presented to a consumer for repeated work. In plugin-wallet mode these roles normally use distinct keys: the wallet keeps the durable owner key, while the browser stores a separate session-purpose Keypair. That does not create a second player, transfer the Backpack, or make the girl in the teaching scene an authority; both roles still refer back to the same owner.

The record address is P_session = PDA(PlayerProgram, ["session", owner, sessionAuthority]). The literal session seed and both public keys are ordered inputs under the Player Program ID. Reusing the same owner and sessionAuthority selects the same PDA, although matching an address alone does not guarantee that refresh will pass its stored relationship checks. Changing only the session authority selects a different PDA for the same player.

The important boundary is outside that PDA. A session Keypair is an ordinary Solana signer, not a NiceChunk-only key. It can create a fresh signature for an independent System Program transfer without referencing PlayerSession and without invoking the Player Program. Session expiry, allowedActions, maxActions, and actionCount are not a key sandbox and do not protect the SOL held at the session address; those fields matter only to code that deliberately reads them. The PlayerSession record expires, but the Keypair, address, private bytes, and ordinary signing ability do not.

Local Game Wallet changes the role arrangement. Its same long-lived key fills both roles: owner = sessionAuthority, so the same key acts in both account positions and derives PDA(PlayerProgram, ["session", owner, owner]). Successful setup can create or refresh that on-chain PDA without a separate session-purpose signing secret. The client flag usesGameplaySession: false only says there is no separate gameplay Keypair; by itself it does not prove that the PDA exists or that setup succeeded.

facts.detail: The public identity checked against PlayerProfile and stored at byte offset 12 in PlayerSession.

facts.label: Working signer role
facts.detail: A plugin browser Keypair or, in Local Game Wallet mode, the same long-lived owner key.

facts.label: Deterministic address
facts.detail: Both public keys affect the address; private bytes are never PDA seeds.

facts.label: Plugin-wallet arrangement
facts.detail: The reviewed repository plugin flow uses a durable wallet identity plus a separate browser-held session signer.

facts.label: Local Game Wallet arrangement
facts.detail: The same key fills both roles and is long-lived rather than a separate session-purpose wallet.

facts.label: Offline transfer proof
facts.value: System Program · 1 lamport · valid signature · 0 broadcasts
facts.detail: The deterministic audit constructed and verified an ordinary transfer without outputting a private key, referencing PlayerSession, or contacting a network.

callout.title: Treat the session secret like a funded hot key
callout.body: The pass can narrow what audited NiceChunk consumers accept, but it cannot stop the same private key from signing a different transaction message. A signature is not reusable across arbitrary messages; control of the key is what creates another valid signature. Keep its SOL exposure small and protect browser access.

visual.alt: NiceChunk coastal locker scene where the canonical boy keeps one durable owner seal secured above one Backpack while holding a separate copper working key, and the canonical girl points to the single player's work area without receiving either key.
visual.caption: One locker, one Backpack, a secured durable seal, and a hand-held copper key keep two credentials attached to one player's work area. The generated physical metaphor does not prove key ownership, derive a PDA, reveal private bytes, establish browser origin, or show that either key signed a transaction.

sections.title: A 15-byte request names the limits, and both signer roles must approve it
paragraphs: The Player Program dispatches instruction tag 4 to create_or_refresh_player_session. The reviewed repository and SDK builders serialize 15 bytes in total: one tag byte plus a 14-byte payload made from an eight-byte expiresAt, a two-byte allowedActions mask, and a four-byte maxActions. In the full data, tag starts at offset 0, expiresAt at offset 1, allowedActions at offset 9, and maxActions at offset 11. Six accounts accompany it: owner, sessionAuthority, PlayerProfile, PlayerSession, GlobalConfig, and System Program.

The owner must be a writable signer, and the session authority must be a signer. In the reviewed direct browser transaction, distinct keys normally produce two transaction signatures: one from the wallet owner and one from the browser Keypair. When the same public key occupies both roles, one transaction signature can satisfy both account-role checks. The contract itself observes AccountInfo.is_signer; a CPI PDA signer can have different mechanics, so the two-private-key explanation is specific to this direct client flow.

The program rejects expiresAt unless it is in the future, meaning expiresAt > chain Clock time. It also rejects a zero mask, any bit outside the two declared bits, and maxActions = 0. The reviewed repository and retained live chain asset use an eight-hour request, allowedActions mask 6 (0b0110), and maxActions 10,000. Those are client defaults: the contract does not impose an eight-hour maximum.

If the canonical PDA already belongs to Player Program, tag 4 can refresh it only after the existing owner, authority, playerProfile, and globalConfig all match the current inputs and the current player-v7 Profile precondition passes. Otherwise the owner funds allocation of a new canonical PDA. Selecting the same address is therefore not a guarantee of rewrite success. Repository Session builders request PlayerSession writable for setup; their later Session-aware consumers request it read-only.

facts.label: Instruction selector
facts.detail: The first byte selects create_or_refresh_player_session.

facts.label: Production data size
facts.detail: 1 tag + 8 expiresAt + 2 allowedActions + 4 maxActions.

facts.label: Instruction-data offsets
facts.detail: The handler receives only the final 14 bytes after dispatch removes the tag.

facts.detail: The owner also pays for setup fees, optional funding, profile creation, and Session allocation.

facts.label: Authority requirement
facts.detail: Possession of the public address alone cannot create or refresh its Session.

facts.label: Distinct keys
facts.value: two different public keys require both signatures
facts.detail: The plugin wallet and session-purpose Keypair approve the same setup transaction.

facts.label: Matching keys
facts.value: one key and one signature satisfy both roles

callout.title: One wallet prompt does not mean one signer
callout.body: A plugin may show one owner approval dialog, while the browser also applies the session-purpose Keypair signature to that same transaction. The program independently requires both signer roles.

visual.alt: NiceChunk registration press where the canonical boy places a durable owner seal and separate copper session key into two sockets around one blank record while the canonical girl observes an alternate fixture where one key spans both role sockets.
visual.caption: The two-socket press represents two required signer roles, and the alternate spanning key represents one public key filling both account positions. These generated mechanisms do not collect signatures, serialize tag 4, prove atomic execution, create or refresh an account, place a private key on-chain, or show a successful Local Game Wallet transaction.

sections.title: The chain publishes the pass record; the browser keeps the secret that can use it
paragraphs: PlayerSession is a fixed 184-byte public record beginning with NCKSES01. Anyone reading the account can see its public owner, session authority, linked profile and configuration, world, mask, timestamps, and stored counters. Public keys identify accounts; they are not private signing bytes. The record cannot reveal the secret half of the session Keypair.

Plugin-wallet mode stores that private half in localStorage under nicechunk.session.v1.<owner>. The JSON contains the public key, secretKey encoded as Base64, expiresAt, and savedAt. Base64 is not encryption or protection; it is a reversible text encoding. Browser code or an extension able to read this origin's storage may copy the secret. The public account and private secret therefore have different visibility and different risks.

The table below follows the Rust pack order and matching SDK decoder. Offsets count from the first byte, so owner starts at byte 12 after eight magic bytes, two version bytes, one bump byte, and one active byte. The Devnet capture calls byte 11 initialized; the SDK calls it active; current consumers ignore it under either name. Multi-byte integers use the implementation's little-endian readers and writers. Refresh changes mask, expiry, updatedSlot, and maxActions and resets actionCount, while createdSlot and createdAt remain creation metadata.

A read-only capture on 2026-07-18 retained 10 PlayerSession records for 8 unique owners at confirmed Devnet context slot 477236696. RPC getBlockTime for that context slot returned 2026-07-18T21:56:51Z, and all 10 records were expired relative to that retained block time. The capture did not directly read the Clock sysvar used by a consumer. Five records had owner = sessionAuthority and five used distinct authorities, while one owner had three Sessions; public equality does not prove which client created them.

The referenced Profiles were six current v7 records, three historical v2 records, and one historical v3 record. A customized direct contract call that supplies the stored historical Profile can make a matching 773-byte v3 record pass the selected consumer shape, owner, and configuration checks; a 473-byte v2 record fails the 773-byte length gate. Repository-default builders instead derive the current player-v7 Profile, so this custom compatibility does not prove a default Play path can use a historical Session pointer or complete later action rules.

The Session, Profile, and GlobalConfig program-account reads share context slot 477236696. Player Program and Player ProgramData used supplemental nearby context slots at +1 and +2, not the exact shared account context. Their hashes bind only the observed Player deployment bytes—not Chunk, Building, unified Game, Backpack, or Guardian—and do not prove this dirty source tree was reproducibly compiled to the deployment.

This is a single confirmed Devnet snapshot, not a Mainnet claim, finalized guarantee, permanent population total, or proof that anyone possessed a corresponding secret. It did not read session-authority balances or browser storage. The retained context-slot RPC block time supports the expiry classification, while live consumers independently read Clock.unix_timestamp when an instruction executes.

callout.title: Public consistency is not secret possession
callout.body: A canonical address, matching bump, valid mask, and unexpired timestamp can describe a well-formed public record. None of those observations proves that the reader owns or has protected the private session key.

sections.title: Fifteen audited contract Session branches share BREAK_BLOCK; default Play reachability is separate
paragraphs: The source-derived capability matrix identifies 15 audited contract handlers with a PlayerSession-aware branch: five Chunk handlers, eight Building handlers, and two Backpack handlers. All 15 such branches request BREAK_BLOCK bit index 1, whose value or permission mask is 2. Fifteen does not mean or prove that one default Play bundle reaches, exports, or calls every handler; actual reachability depends on the runtime-resolved asset and call graph.

The five Chunk handlers are mine_block, mine_block_with_rewards, fell_tree_with_rewards, batch_mine_with_rewards, and range_mine_with_rewards. Current repository source defines both batch tag 20 and range tag 21 builders but its default bulk call points to range. The retained 2026-07-19 default live chain candidate exposes no recordBulkMineOnChain export and produced zero exact writeUInt8(20, 0) / writeUInt8(21, 0) literal matches; that exact-string search does not exclude equivalent encodings such as variables, hexadecimal bytes, or Buffer constructors. The one versioned dedicated bulk candidate named by the runtime returned 404. A separately probed unversioned bulk URL also returned 404 but was not the nonempty-version runtime's default. These byte observations separate contract capability from default live Play reachability and remain override-dependent.

The eight Building handlers are create_build_site, register_build_site_chunks, begin_building_upload, write_building_shard, finalize_building, cancel_building_upload, migrate_legacy_build_site, and begin_build_site_resize. They are Building instruction tags 0 through 7, and every one uses BREAK_BLOCK action index 1. Building tag 8 is not a ninth Session flow: it publishes a Guardian blueprint through a separate fixed-publisher path. cancel_building_upload can return manifest and shard rent to sessionAuthority, so this bit can authorize a SOL destination as well as construction state.

Backpack routing has two layers. The repository-default gameContext and the retained live chain candidate target the unified Game Program 6CurnvneezBuHwPUnrCiFg1QMWeUF67ufQxYebyr2UP7 for Backpack. Namespace byte 1 wraps the inner Backpack data: single removal is [1, 2, index], and batch removal begins [1, 4, count, ...]. The unified Game Program strips namespace one and delegates the remaining payload to the shared nicechunk_backpack handler.

Each Backpack handler also has two account shapes. Its four-account Session-aware branch reads authority, PlayerProfile, PlayerSession, and Backpack, then requests BREAK_BLOCK bit 1. Its two-account owner-direct branch uses owner signer plus Backpack and bypasses PlayerSession entirely. Repository and retained live chain-client discard builders use the four-account unified-Game form, but the contract's owner-direct form means it would be wrong to say every Backpack removal requires a Session.

Both forms remove a dense packed Backpack slot or reference. remove_resource shifts later fixed-size records left; remove_resources rejects empty, duplicate, or out-of-range indexes and removes valid choices high to low. Neither checks slot kind, type, or category, so material/resource, block, smelting, blueprint, and forged equipment/item references are structurally in scope. The removal only shifts and clears the packed Backpack record; it does not close, modify, or refund an underlying item PDA that the record may reference.

Repository Session-aware builders request all 15 PlayerSession metas read-only, but read-only is not the sole reason actionCount does not change. The handlers borrow or inspect Session data immutably, never read or compare actionCount with maxActions, do not own the Player Program account, and contain no Player Program increment instruction or CPI. Therefore actionCount is not incremented or updated and 10,000 is not a current limit, even though a different transaction could merge account privileges more broadly.

At least six control fields are ignored: the validators do not read or check version, bump, active, worldId, maxActions, or actionCount; createdSlot, updatedSlot, and createdAt also do not authorize actions. Chunk and Building read globalConfig while Backpack's Session view omits it. Chunk and Building require sessionAuthority signer+writable; Backpack's Session branch requires signer but not writable. All require 184 bytes, NCKSES01, and Player Program ownership, but none derives the canonical PlayerSession PDA. This defense-in-depth gap is not proof that an attacker can freely create arbitrary Player-owned accounts.

Historical Profile compatibility is narrower than a length check. A custom direct call that supplies a stored, pointer-matched 773-byte v3 Profile may pass selected Session and Profile predicates; 473-byte v2 fails length. Repository-default builders derive current player-v7 instead, so a historical Profile observation does not mean default Play can reach or use that old Session. The other Profile fields version, bump, initialized, worldId, and canonical Profile PDA are not all enforced by every consumer either.

PLACE_BLOCK is bit index 2 with value 4, but it has zero audited contract consumers. The reviewed repository method and retained live chain candidate return the literal reason chain-placement-disabled before submitting ordinary placement. Storing mask 6 therefore does not make PLACE_BLOCK an on-chain placement permission.

callout.title: Read the consumer, not the permission label
callout.body: A bit name describes a contract branch; the resolved client asset and call site decide whether a player can reach it. Bit 1 spans Session-aware mining, Building, and generic dense Backpack-reference removal branches, while owner-direct Backpack removal bypasses Session and bit 2 has no audited consumer.

visual.caption: The connected quarry, Building yard, and mixed dense sorting bench suggest one permission reaching several kinds of work, while the capped second socket suggests an unwired path. The generated scene does not prove bit values, enumerate 15 handlers, execute Backpack deletion, demonstrate index shifts, or establish chain-placement-disabled.

sections.title: Browser reuse is a heuristic; refresh is conditional, and saving the secret is a second commit
paragraphs: Plugin-wallet mode saves the session-purpose Keypair under nicechunk.session.v1.<owner wallet>. Reuse begins with the copied browser expiresAt and requires localExpiresAt > now + 900 seconds, a 15-minute skew. It derives that authority's PDA, but its on-chain Session test is only that the account has nonempty data. It does not decode or validate Session length, magic, stored owner, authority, Profile pointer, mask, or chain expiresAt before the branch proceeds.

The separately derived canonical-address Profile goes through selected checks: Player Program ownership, 773-byte decodable NCKPLY01 shape, stored owner, and GlobalConfig. The helper reads but does not enforce Profile version or initialized, so calling it a “current v7 Profile” would be too strong. Reuse is therefore a browser convenience heuristic, not the authoritative gate; a consumer can still reject the key for its actual Session, Profile, expiry, permission, or action rules.

Reuse may still ask the owner wallet to sign. The branch calls funding before a complete on-chain Session validation; when balance is below 0.1 SOL, it builds an owner-to-authority System transfer and requests wallet approval. Only a fresh-enough, adequately funded stored key can avoid a new owner transaction. Approving a top-up is not proof that the pass is usable, because a later consumer can reject the Session after the SOL transfer has confirmed.

The same owner and same authority select the same PDA, but address selection does not guarantee a rewrite. Refresh requires the existing record's stored owner, sessionAuthority, playerProfile, and globalConfig to match the tag-4 inputs, requires the old authority to sign, and first requires the current player-v7 Profile PDA. Only then can it replace allowedActions, expiresAt, updatedSlot, and maxActions and reset actionCount. The contract accepts any still-future expiry, so a correctly approved refresh can shorten as well as lengthen time; the repository client normally asks for now + 8 hours.

That pointer rule matters to old accounts. A Session that still stores a historical v2 or v3 Profile pointer hits the same PDA for the same authority but cannot refresh in place against the required current player-v7 pointer. Plugin mode can choose a new authority and create a new PDA pointing at the current Profile, while the old PDA and rent remain. Local Game Wallet fixes authority = owner, so the same-address historical-pointer case has no automatic key-rotation escape in this flow. This is a source-level compatibility boundary, not an observed failure in the retained snapshot, whose five owner-equals-authority records all pointed at current v7.

The repository and SDK tag-4 builders mark an existing PlayerProfile read-only. Player Program's ensure_player_profile_current fast path checks only length, NCKPLY01 magic, owner, and GlobalConfig; it does not enforce version or initialized. If a Player-Program-owned, existing nonempty current player-v7 PDA fails one of those selected checks, the repair branch can top up rent, realloc to 773 bytes, and repack—operations needing writable privilege. An older layout is only in this conditional repair-risk scope when one of the selected length, magic, owner, or GlobalConfig checks fails. A missing or new Profile receives a writable initialize instruction that normally raises transaction-level privilege, but an existing nonempty Profile does not. Automatic repair may therefore fail unless another instruction makes it writable. This audit observed no production failure evidence; it records a conditional integration risk, not proof that every old Profile fails.

Rotation is not revocation. Missing or damaged plugin storage leads to a new Keypair and different PDA. Creating that new PDA does not stop, deactivate, close, or refund the old one, and it locks another allocation's rent. The old record can remain usable with its old secret until its own expiry and gates pass. One Devnet owner having three Sessions is only a coexistence snapshot, not proof that all three were simultaneously usable.

Chain setup and browser secret persistence are non-atomic. The client broadcasts and waits for the owner setup transaction before calling storeGameplaySession; then localStorage.setItem performs a separate browser write. RPC confirmation ambiguity, page closure, unavailable storage, quota or permission errors, or a thrown setItem can leave a funded authority and PlayerSession on chain without a durable local secret. A later retry may generate another authority, transfer another target balance, and allocate another rent-funded PDA. No one outcome is guaranteed, but the two commits can strand repeated balances and rent.

Local Game Wallet has a different reuse shortcut. A five-minute in-memory ready cache can skip RPC and report a newly recomputed now + 8 hours instead of the record's real expiresAt. Five minutes bounds only the no-RPC cache window, not the expiry overstatement: a cached existing Session could have been just above the 15-minute freshness floor, so the synthetic local time may materially exceed the chain record. Outside the cache, its RPC freshness helper checks Player ownership, 184-byte magic, stored owner and authority, and expiresAt > now + 900, but does not cross-check Session playerProfile, globalConfig, allowedActions, version, active, or maxActions. The consumer remains final.

Finally, play-chain-adapter.js can dynamically import a global override or localStorage nicechunk.chainModuleUrl before its normal candidates. Dynamic import executes and evaluates module code before the adapter checks whether expected exports exist. Never paste or configure an untrusted module URL: same-origin-accessible browser secrets may be exposed to code that runs. This is a capability and trust warning, not evidence that an override exploit or compromise has occurred.

callout.title: Rotation without revocation leaves overlap
callout.body: For an attacker holding only an independent plugin session key, that old record's expiry ends its checked NiceChunk access. It does not erase the key or SOL. If the owner or Local Game Wallet key is compromised, the attacker can approve a new or refreshed future Session, so expiry is not the final compromise stop-loss.

sections.eyebrow: FEES, BALANCES, AND PERSISTENT LOCAL KEYS
sections.title: The 0.1 SOL policy is a refill trigger, not a spending cap or key restriction
paragraphs: A separate session-purpose Keypair needs SOL in its ordinary sessionAuthority System account to pay transaction fees and, where an action creates accounts, rent funding. The reviewed repository source and retained live chain candidate use a minimum of 100,000,000 lamports, or 0.1 SOL, and clamp the configured target to at least that value. Below 0.1 SOL the owner may be asked to transfer toward the target; at or above the threshold, a higher target is not automatically filled.

That top-up target is not a cap, limit, budget, escrow, or on-chain authorization rule. PlayerSession stores neither target nor balance. The Session record can expire, but the Keypair, address, and private signing bytes do not expire, erase, or revoke themselves. Expiry, allowedActions, maxActions, and actionCount do not protect or restrict the address's SOL and cannot stop the same key from signing an ordinary SystemProgram.transfer that never consults Player Program.

Repository chain code and the retained live candidate use the session Keypair as fee payer for supported later calls. Fees and account rent can reduce its balance, while any controller of the private key can create a different ordinary transfer signature. The offline one-lamport System Program proof verifies that signing shape without a real balance, live blockhash, broadcast, or fund movement, so it proves ordinary signing capability rather than successful execution.

Do not combine authority balance with PlayerSession PDA rent. The retained Devnet accounts each held 2,171,520 lamports as the observed rent-exempt minimum for a separate 184-byte PDA; the snapshot did not query authority balances. A new authority creates another PDA and locks another rent allocation. Because the reviewed Player Program has no Session close path, expiry, rotation, and logout do not refund that rent.

Logout also does not sweep the authority's SOL. If best-effort cleanup successfully deletes the only nicechunk.session.v1.<owner> copy and no backup exists, normal UI flow no longer has the private bytes needed to move the old address's remaining SOL. Reconnecting can create and fund a new key instead. This loss is not guaranteed—copies may exist or deletion may fail—but there is no automatic recovery or pre-logout drain, so 0.1 SOL is not a guaranteed maximum loss.

Local Game Wallet is a different and larger risk shape. Its owner = sessionAuthority, so one persistent local key can satisfy both setup roles and later wallet authority. The browser stores that long-lived private key as an unencrypted plain Base58 string under separate Local Game Wallet keys. Its full wallet balance is exposed to that key, and plugin-session logout does not delete it. usesGameplaySession: false says only that there is no separate gameplay Keypair; it does not prove an on-chain PDA exists. After successful setup, the flow can use PDA(PlayerProgram, ["session", owner, owner]).

If only an independent plugin session key leaks, the old record's chain expiry eventually blocks its audited NiceChunk branches. If the owner wallet secret or Local Game Wallet key leaks, the attacker can occupy both required setup roles or choose a new authority and create or refresh a future Session. Session expiry is therefore not final containment for owner-key compromise, and short expiries cannot repair loss of the long-lived key.

facts.label: Minimum refill policy
facts.detail: Present in reviewed repository and retained live chain bytes; it is not a field in the 184-byte Session.

facts.label: Separate Session PDA rent
facts.value: 2,171,520 lamports in the retained Devnet snapshot
facts.detail: Observed on each 184-byte PlayerSession PDA; a new authority allocates another PDA, and no reviewed Session close refunds it.

facts.label: Later signer
facts.detail: The chain client sets transaction.feePayer to the Keypair public key and signs with that Keypair.

facts.label: Independent signer power
facts.detail: No PlayerSession account or Player Program invocation is required by that separate transaction.

facts.label: Plugin mode

callout.title: Funding policy reduces friction, not cryptographic authority
callout.body: A smaller balance can reduce exposure, but only key custody and transaction construction control an ordinary address outside Session-aware consumers. Expiry and masks do not wrap SOL in a protected account, and owner-key compromise can create another future Session.

visual.alt: NiceChunk funding bench where the canonical boy moves unmarked fee ingots from an owner chest to an exposed box attached to the copper session key, which fits both a session turnstile and ordinary supply lock, while the canonical girl warns beside a one-key dual-role fixture.
visual.caption: The exposed fee box, ordinary copper key, separate locks, owner chest, and dual-role fixture teach funding and signer scope without using literal currency UI. The generated props do not identify SOL, measure fees or rent, perform a transfer, prove unrestricted signing power, establish loss, or verify the Local Game Wallet relationship.

sections.title: Expiry closes checked gates; logout, key rotation, and Guardian do not revoke the account
paragraphs: A Chunk, Building, or Backpack Session branch rejects when expiresAt <= Clock.unix_timestamp. That closes this old public record's checked gate; it does not expire, erase, revoke, or zero the Keypair, address, private bytes, or SOL. The copied browser time is only a hint and cannot make a chain-expired record valid.

The reviewed Player Program has no close, revoke, or deactivate instruction for PlayerSession and no owner-only kill switch. Refresh of a distinct old authority requires that old authority to sign; replacement authority creates another PDA without changing or refunding the old one. The owner therefore cannot immediately close a stolen independent Session through this interface.

Expiry is meaningful only after naming which secret leaked. If an attacker holds only an independent plugin session key, expiry is the cutoff for that old record's audited NiceChunk branches, although the key can still sign ordinary transactions and control remaining SOL. If the owner wallet or Local Game Wallet key is stolen or compromised, that key can approve a new authority or, when owner = sessionAuthority, satisfy both setup roles and create or refresh another future Session. Expiry is not a final or complete compromise stop-loss for the long-lived owner key.

Logout is best-effort browser cleanup, not a verified deletion. clearWalletSession first attempts four wallet-identity removals, then tries to enumerate localStorage. If storage.length or storage.key enumeration throws, it returns and skips the runtime-prefix pass. When enumeration succeeds, it attempts every key beginning nicechunk.session.v1., nicechunk.equippedBackpack.v1., nicechunk.sessionFundingLamports.v1., or nicechunk.sessionFundingAcknowledged.v1. Each removeItem error is caught and swallowed, and the helper does not read back or confirm success.

The prefix pass is not limited to the connected wallet. A successful scan can delete another owner's only or last browser copy of a plugin session secret, while an error can leave a supposedly cleared secret behind. Logout therefore does not guarantee deletion, and its cross-owner reach is a risk rather than proof that every matching key was removed.

Logout does not alter on-chain PlayerSession state, send a revoke, close or refund the Session PDA, or drain, recover, return, or refund the authority address's SOL. It also does not delete the separately stored Local Game Wallet private key. If the only plugin secret is successfully removed before its balance is swept, the normal UI may lose access to that SOL even while the public Session and rent remain. A copied key elsewhere may keep using an unexpired old record if its gates pass.

Guardian is independent of this lifecycle. Realtime HELLO sends an unsigned public wallet hint plus nonce—not possession proof, PlayerSession, or wallet signature. Guardian Program manages registry, operators, endpoints, and blueprints; Guardian does not issue, validate, refresh, or revoke PlayerSession. Building tag 8 is a separate fixed-publisher Guardian blueprint path, not one of Building's eight Session-aware tags.

At confirmed Devnet context slot 477236696, the retained 2026-07-18 RPC getBlockTime value classified all 10 Session records as expired. This was not a direct Clock sysvar read. Session/Profile/Config reads shared that slot, while Player Program and ProgramData hash reads used supplemental nearby +1 and +2 contexts and cover only Player deployment bytes. Expired records remaining visible show allocation can outlive authorization, but this one confirmed Devnet snapshot is not Mainnet, an ongoing monitor, or a permanent population count.

facts.label: Consumer expiry rule
facts.detail: The decision uses Clock.unix_timestamp inside each audited consumer.

facts.label: Immediate owner revocation
facts.value: no close · no revoke · no deactivate instruction
facts.detail: The reviewed Player Program dispatch exposes create or refresh but no Session lifecycle kill handler.

facts.label: Old-key refresh
facts.detail: Owner consent alone cannot extend or overwrite a distinct old authority's canonical Session.

facts.label: Logout storage scope
facts.value: best-effort attempts across four runtime prefixes and all owner suffixes
facts.detail: Enumeration failure skips the prefix pass; each removeItem error is swallowed and no success read-back occurs.

facts.label: Logout chain effect
facts.detail: No revoke, Session PDA close or rent refund, session-authority SOL drain, or other on-chain Session transaction is sent.

facts.label: Local Game Wallet after logout
facts.detail: Plugin-session cleanup does not clear the separate Base58 secret.

facts.label: Guardian input
facts.detail: No PlayerSession, session authority, session secret, or wallet signature is sent in HELLO.

facts.label: Retained Devnet boundary
facts.detail: Expiry used context-slot RPC getBlockTime rather than a direct Clock sysvar read; Player deployment hashes used nearby +1/+2 contexts.

facts.label: Successful cross-owner cleanup risk
facts.value: another owner's sole browser secret can be removed
facts.detail: That deletion still does not sweep its SOL, refund its Session rent, or remove copies outside this browser origin.

facts.label: Long-lived-key compromise
facts.value: owner or Local Game Wallet key can authorize another future Session
facts.detail: Expiry contains only the old record used by an attacker who lacks the owner key; it is not complete owner-key recovery.

callout.title: Know which key you are trying to contain
callout.body: Short expiry helps when only an independent session key leaks. It does not repair an owner or Local Game Wallet compromise. Protect both secrets, keep session-purpose balances modest, recover funds before logout when possible, and remember that logout only attempts to remove copies it can enumerate.

visual.alt: NiceChunk late-afternoon waystation where the canonical boy clears copper keys from every open session cubby while a copied key and intact stone record remain elsewhere, the canonical girl checks a closed expired gate, and an unconnected Guardian tower stands far away.
visual.caption: The empty hourglass, closed gate, intact public record, prefix-wide cubby cleanup, untouched long-lived key, surviving copy, two-socket refresh press, and distant tower separate expiry, local logout, missing revocation, and Guardian. The generated scene does not execute any check, delete real storage, move SOL, close an account, preserve a real secret, or prove Guardian independence.

technicalHeading.title: Separate the account predicate, the ordinary key, and the browser lifecycle
technicalHeading.summary: The formulas translate signer roles, byte layout, permission bits, consumer reads, reuse, funding, and expiry into plain tests. Literal excerpts separate the repository production /play/ source route, the runtime-resolved live Nginx assets retained on 2026-07-19 UTC, contract capability, SDK serialization, offline derivation, and Devnet account snapshot.

formulas.title: Two roles describe one player's session
formulas.expression: roles = { owner: long-lived identity, sessionAuthority: signer presented to consumers }
formulas.ariaLabel: A Player Session names one long-lived owner role and one session-authority signing role for the same player.
formulas.explanation: The public keys may be different in plugin-wallet mode or identical in Local Game Wallet mode. Account roles are logical positions; they do not guarantee two different physical secrets.

terms.meaning: The persistent public key checked by PlayerProfile.
The signer public key compared by a session-aware consumer.

formulas.title: Canonical PlayerSession address
formulas.expression: P_session = PDA(PlayerProgram, ["session", owner, sessionAuthority])
formulas.ariaLabel: The Session PDA is derived under the Player Program from the word session, the owner, and the session-authority public key in that order.
formulas.explanation: The two public keys are address inputs. Keeping both identical inputs returns the same PDA; replacing the authority returns a different PDA and does not write the earlier account.

terms.meaning: The deterministic 184-byte public account address.
The NiceChunk Player Program whose ID defines the PDA domain.

formulas.title: Direct browser signer count follows distinct public keys
formulas.expression: setupSigners = { owner, sessionAuthority }; owner != sessionAuthority ⇒ 2 signatures; owner = sessionAuthority ⇒ 1 signature satisfies both roles
formulas.ariaLabel: Distinct owner and authority keys require two signatures, while one matching key can satisfy both signer account roles with one signature.
formulas.explanation: This describes the reviewed direct browser transaction. The contract actually checks is_signer on both account positions; equal metas can rely on one transaction signature, while a CPI PDA signer may use invoke_signed instead of a private key.

terms.meaning: The public keys whose account roles must be marked as signers.

formulas.title: Reviewed repository setup instruction size
formulas.expression: setupDataBytes = 1 tag + 8 expiresAt + 2 allowedActions + 4 maxActions = 15 bytes
formulas.ariaLabel: The reviewed repository create-or-refresh builder uses one tag byte and a fourteen-byte payload, for fifteen bytes total.
formulas.explanation: Tag 4 selects the handler. The contract receives the remaining fourteen bytes as payload and reads the timestamp, mask, and stored maximum from fixed offsets.

terms.meaning: The one-byte instruction selector whose current value is 4.

formulas.title: Core create-or-refresh acceptance
formulas.expression: setupCoreOK = ownerSigned AND sessionSigned AND expiresAt > Clock.unix_timestamp AND allowedActions != 0 AND (allowedActions & ~6) = 0 AND maxActions > 0
formulas.ariaLabel: Setup requires both signer roles, a future expiry, a nonzero mask containing only values two and four, and a nonzero stored maximum.
formulas.explanation: PDA, ownership, Profile, GlobalConfig, System Program, and writable-account checks also run. The expression isolates the signer and payload requirements most visible to players.

terms.meaning: The union of the only two declared setup permission bits.

formulas.title: Default permission mask
formulas.expression: defaultAllowedActions = (1 << 1) | (1 << 2) = 2 | 4 = 6 = 0b0110
formulas.ariaLabel: The reviewed repository and retained live client bytes combine BREAK BLOCK at bit index one and PLACE BLOCK at bit index two into decimal mask six.
formulas.explanation: A mask records which bits are present. It does not prove that a corresponding consumer exists, and current PLACE_BLOCK has no audited consumer.

terms.meaning: Move one binary bit left by n positions.

formulas.title: Fixed record length
formulas.ariaLabel: The sixteen packed fields add up exactly to the fixed one-hundred-eighty-four-byte Player Session record.
formulas.explanation: The sum follows the literal Rust writer and the SDK decoder order. Offsets are byte positions inside this exact fixed-length public account.

terms.meaning: The byte length of each Solana public key field.

formulas.title: Chunk and Building consumer gate
formulas.expression: gateCB(a,t) = ownedByPlayerProgram AND length=184 AND magic=NCKSES01 AND sessionSignedWritable AND authorityMatch AND profileMatch AND globalConfigMatch AND expiresAt > t AND (allowedActions & (1 << a)) != 0
formulas.ariaLabel: Chunk and Building require a Player-owned one-hundred-eighty-four-byte NCKSES01 record, a signed writable authority, matching Profile and Global Config, future expiry, and requested action bit.
formulas.explanation: Their helpers then validate the owner-backed Profile and continue into action-specific rules. This expression includes the fixed length and magic predicates but not every downstream rule; they do not derive the canonical Session PDA or read the six omitted control fields.

terms.meaning: The requested action-bit index, currently fixed to 1 in these consumers.
Clock.unix_timestamp read during the instruction.

formulas.title: Backpack consumer gate
formulas.expression: gateBackpack(a,t) = ownedByPlayerProgram AND length=184 AND magic=NCKSES01 AND sessionSigned AND authorityMatch AND profileMatch AND expiresAt > t AND (allowedActions & (1 << a)) != 0
formulas.ariaLabel: Backpack checks a Player-owned one-hundred-eighty-four-byte NCKSES01 record, the signed authority, matching Profile, future expiry, and the requested action bit before owner-backed deletion.
formulas.explanation: Unlike Chunk and Building, Backpack requires the authority to sign but not to be writable, and its Session view does not compare GlobalConfig. It later validates Profile owner and the owner's Backpack PDA; this expression is the shared Session gate rather than every deletion rule.

terms.meaning: The shared PlayerSession part of either dense removal handler.

formulas.title: Highlighted control fields omitted by every audited consumer
formulas.expression: unreadControlFields = { version, bump, active, worldId, maxActions, actionCount }; alsoNotConsumed = { createdSlot, updatedSlot, createdAt }
formulas.ariaLabel: Every audited Session view omits six highlighted control fields, and its creation and update metadata also do not participate in authorization.
formulas.explanation: A stored field has no enforcement effect unless a consumer reads and applies it. Backpack additionally omits globalConfig, while Chunk and Building compare that field.

terms.meaning: The six control fields explicitly tracked as ignored by all three consumer views.
Creation and update metadata retained in the record but not used by the consumer gates.

formulas.title: Audited contract Session-branch total
formulas.expression: auditedSessionBranches = 5 Chunk + 8 Building + 2 Backpack = 15
formulas.ariaLabel: Five Chunk handlers, eight Building handlers, and two Backpack handlers expose fifteen audited contract Session-aware branches.
formulas.explanation: This is a contract and repository-builder capability surface, not proof that one default Play runtime reaches all fifteen. Every counted Session branch requests BREAK_BLOCK; Backpack also keeps owner-direct branches outside this count.

terms.meaning: Handlers with a traced PlayerSession-aware account branch, independent of default live client reachability.

formulas.title: Actual permission consumption
formulas.expression: BREAK_BLOCK = 1 << 1 = 2 → 15 audited contract Session branches; PLACE_BLOCK = 1 << 2 = 4 → 0 audited contract consumers
formulas.ariaLabel: BREAK BLOCK value two is requested by all fifteen audited contract Session branches, while PLACE BLOCK value four is requested by none.
formulas.explanation: The contract matrix, not the friendly name, defines capability. Default runtime reachability remains asset-dependent, and reviewed ordinary placement returns chain-placement-disabled before a Session consumer is submitted.

terms.meaning: Maps a stored bit to the number of audited consumer paths using it.

formulas.title: No implemented action-count ceiling
formulas.expression: builders request read-only AND handlers only inspect Session AND actionCount is unread AND no Player increment CPI ⇒ actionCountAfter = actionCountBefore; actionCount < maxActions predicate = absent
formulas.ariaLabel: Read-only builder requests, read-only handler logic, no action-count read, and no Player increment call together leave action count unchanged and unenforced.
formulas.explanation: Read-only account meta is not the sole barrier because transaction privileges can merge. The independent handler behavior, Player ownership boundary, missing comparison, and missing increment instruction establish the current non-enforcement.

terms.meaning: No such comparison exists in the audited authorization paths.

formulas.title: Unified Game Backpack envelope
formulas.expression: single outer data = [namespace 1, inner tag 2, index]; batch outer data = [namespace 1, inner tag 4, count, indexes...]
formulas.ariaLabel: The default unified Game route prefixes Backpack single and batch removal data with namespace byte one before inner tags two and four.
formulas.explanation: Game Program removes the first namespace byte and delegates the remaining payload to shared Backpack code. This explains why outer live bytes and inner Backpack handler tags are both correct descriptions of one route.

terms.meaning: The unified Game selector for Backpack instructions.

formulas.title: Stored ten thousand is not a cap
formulas.expression: stored maxActions = 10,000 != enforced successful-action limit
formulas.ariaLabel: The browser writes ten thousand into max actions, but current consumers do not enforce ten thousand as a successful-action cap.
formulas.explanation: The value is visible public data and must be nonzero at setup. Without a consumer read, comparison, and counter update, it remains descriptive storage rather than a ceiling.

terms.meaning: Means the two ideas must not be treated as equivalent.

formulas.title: Plugin browser reuse heuristic
formulas.expression: reuseClient = storedKey AND localExpiresAt > now + 900 AND sessionPdaNonemptyData AND canonicalProfileSelectedGate
formulas.ariaLabel: The plugin browser reuses a stored key from copied local time, nonempty Session data, and selected canonical Profile shape and relationship checks.
formulas.explanation: Session data is not decoded here. The Profile predicate checks program owner, 773 bytes, NCKPLY01, stored owner, and GlobalConfig but does not enforce version or initialized. Consumers remain final.

terms.meaning: The current fifteen-minute refresh skew in seconds.

formulas.title: Refresh and rotation address outcomes
formulas.expression: same owner + same authority ⇒ same PDA selection; rewrite only if stored owner, authority, Profile, Config and current player-v7 preconditions match; new authority ⇒ different PDA AND old PDA unchanged
formulas.ariaLabel: The same key tuple selects the same address but rewrites only after stored relationships pass; a new authority selects another PDA and leaves the old account unchanged.
formulas.explanation: The repository normally asks for now plus eight hours, but any still-future expiry may shorten or lengthen time. A historical Profile pointer can make same-PDA refresh fail; a new authority adds another rent-funded account.

terms.meaning: The replacement-key transaction does not pass the older account as writable.

formulas.title: Chain setup and secret storage are separate commits
formulas.expression: wallet broadcast/confirmation → PlayerSession and funding may exist; then localStorage.setItem(secret) → browser recovery copy may exist
formulas.ariaLabel: The owner transaction can reach the chain before a separate browser write durably saves the new session secret.
formulas.explanation: There is no atomic rollback joining these two systems. Confirmation ambiguity, page closure, unavailable storage, quota failure, or setItem exception can leave chain state and funds without a saved browser secret; retries may create another key and PDA.

terms.meaning: Execution order, not a guarantee that the later step succeeds.

formulas.title: Local Game Wallet cache reports synthetic expiry
formulas.expression: readyCacheAge < 300 seconds ⇒ skip RPC and report expiresAt = now + 28,800; on-chain expiresAt remains unchanged
formulas.ariaLabel: A five-minute Local Game Wallet ready-cache hit skips RPC and reports now plus eight hours without changing or reading the Session record.
formulas.explanation: Five minutes limits only how long RPC is bypassed. A cached existing record may have been near the fifteen-minute freshness floor, so the synthetic reported expiry can materially overstate chain expiry by far more than five minutes.

terms.meaning: The in-memory cache lifetime in seconds, not an expiry-error bound.

formulas.title: Current top-up policy
formulas.expression: topUp = balance < 0.1 SOL ? max(0, configuredTarget - balance) : 0
formulas.ariaLabel: The browser tops up only below zero-point-one SOL and then fills toward the configured target, which is not a spending cap.
formulas.explanation: The minimum and target are browser policy, not an escrow or cap. During reuse, this top-up can request another owner-wallet approval before the Session has been fully validated, so funding does not prove usability.

terms.meaning: The confirmed SOL balance of the separate plugin session address.

formulas.title: Ordinary transfer bypasses the pass record
formulas.expression: ordinaryTransferSignatureValid = sessionKeySignature AND SystemProgram.transfer; PlayerSessionReferenced = false; PlayerProgramInvoked = false
formulas.ariaLabel: A valid session-key signature can authorize an ordinary System Program transfer without referencing Player Session or invoking Player Program.
formulas.explanation: The retained proof uses a synthetic unfunded key, stays offline, sends no transaction, and exposes no private key. It proves signature capability, not executable balance or real fund movement.

terms.meaning: Cryptographic construction and signature verification, not a broadcast result.

formulas.title: Rotation allocates rent without closing the old account
formulas.expression: new sessionAuthority ⇒ new PlayerSession PDA + another rent allocation; reviewed close/refund instruction = absent
formulas.ariaLabel: A new authority creates another rent-funded Session PDA, while the reviewed Player Program has no Session close or rent-refund instruction.
formulas.explanation: The retained Devnet minimum was 2,171,520 lamports for each 184-byte Session. Expiry, logout, and key replacement do not reclaim an older PDA's lamports under the reviewed lifecycle.

terms.meaning: A separate account balance, not the temporary authority's 0.1 SOL refill target.

formulas.title: Logout is a best-effort attempt
formulas.expression: logout = attempt identity removals; if enumeration succeeds, attempt every matching prefix removal; per-key exceptions are swallowed; success is not verified
formulas.ariaLabel: Logout tries identity and cross-owner prefix removals, but enumeration or individual deletion failures can leave records behind and no success check follows.
formulas.explanation: Even a successful local secret deletion sends no chain instruction, drains no authority SOL, closes no Session PDA, and leaves the separate Local Game Wallet secret untouched.

terms.meaning: A requested local operation whose success is neither guaranteed nor verified.

formulas.title: Consumer expiry rule
formulas.expression: authorizedAtUse = expiresAt > Clock.unix_timestamp
formulas.ariaLabel: A current consumer accepts the time portion only while expires at is strictly greater than the chain Clock timestamp.
formulas.explanation: When expiresAt is equal to or below chain time, the checked Session path rejects. The public account may remain allocated after this authority ends.

terms.meaning: The chain-provided Unix timestamp inside the program.

formulas.title: Retained Devnet observation
formulas.expression: snapshot(2026-07-18, confirmed slot 477236696, RPC getBlockTime) = 10 PlayerSessions, 8 unique owners, 10 expired relative to retained block time
formulas.ariaLabel: One confirmed Devnet snapshot on July eighteenth at slot four-seven-seven-two-three-six-six-nine-six retained ten Sessions for eight owners, all expired.
formulas.explanation: RPC getBlockTime supplied 2026-07-18T21:56:51Z; the capture did not directly read Clock sysvar. Session/Profile/Config shared the context slot, while Player Program and ProgramData hashes used supplemental +1/+2 slots and cover only Player deployment bytes.

terms.meaning: One shared RPC context whose retained bytes can be checked offline.

evidence.claim: Loads ./main.js as the production module entry for /play/ and contains the current player-facing session-funding explanation.
Imports the modular Play chain adapter, wallet-session controller, player synchronization, placement, mining, and Guardian runtime used by the production route.
Forwards ordinary placement to recordBlockPlacementOnChain and normalizes its current non-submission result.
Implements the visible wallet lifecycle, funding presentation, best-effort provider disconnect, local cleanup call, and login redirect.
Defines the four wallet identity keys, four runtime cache prefixes, and prefix-wide logout scan across all owner suffixes.
Encodes Guardian HELLO with a public wallet hint, chunk coordinates, and nonce but no PlayerSession or wallet signature.
Creates the Guardian realtime client from walletAddress and presentation context without accepting PlayerSession input.

Implements current eight-hour duration, fifteen-minute skew, mask 6, stored maximum 10,000, 0.1 SOL policy, owner-scoped secret storage, reuse, setup, disabled placement, and ordinary Keypair signing.
Stores the long-lived Local Game Wallet secret as Base58 in browser storage and provides the Keypair that fills owner and sessionAuthority roles.
Publishes matching Session constants, PDA derivation, 15-byte tag-four instruction builder, signer metas, and the complete fixed-layout decoder.
Defines Session magic, version, seed, permission bits, 184-byte pack order, refresh mutation, counter reset, and stored relationship checks.
Dispatches tag 4, requires both signer roles, checks payload and future chain time, derives the canonical PDA, and exposes no Session close, revoke, or deactivate handler.
Defines Chunk's selected-field PlayerSession view and validates authority, Profile, GlobalConfig, expiry, and action bit without reading six stored fields.
Dispatches the five audited Session-aware mining and tree handlers and passes action index 1 through the shared read-only Session validation path.
Defines Building's selected-field PlayerSession view with the same authority, Profile, GlobalConfig, expiry, and action-bit checks.
Dispatches all Building tags 0–7 through the shared action-index-1 path while tag 8 publishes Guardian blueprint state through a separate fixed-publisher CPI.
Defines Backpack's Session view plus dense single and batch removal that shift records without checking slot kind, type, or category.
Dispatches session-aware remove_resource and remove_resources using BREAK_BLOCK and mutates the owner-backed Backpack while keeping PlayerSession read-only.
Defines unified Game namespace byte one and delegates its remaining instruction payload to the shared NiceChunk Backpack handler.
Implements Guardian registry, endpoint, blueprint, and operator handlers without referencing PlayerSession or providing its lifecycle operations.
Deterministically derives byte layout, setup defaults, selected consumer reads, 15-handler matrix, lifecycle boundaries, Guardian independence, and an offline ordinary System transfer proof.
Retains the zero-network derived results, source hashes, 15 read-only consumers, no implemented action ceiling, no PLACE consumer, no revoke path, and valid ordinary transfer signature.
Captures read-only Devnet program accounts at a shared context and checks retained bytes, PDAs, distributions, privacy boundaries, and offline reproduction.
Retains one confirmed Devnet snapshot at context slot 477236696 with ten canonical Session records for eight owners, all expired at that chain time.
Captures fixed public NiceChunk HTTPS resources, hashes decoded response bodies, and checks the retained entry, runtime, fallback, candidate, and privacy boundaries offline.
Retains the 2026-07-19 UTC static production observation inside the 2026-07-18 documentation audit batch with path, response, hash, literal, and non-execution boundaries.
Configures the current runtime chain cluster as Devnet and records the Player, Chunk, Building, Backpack, and Core program IDs used by the capture.
Pins the Player Program's expected Core and Backpack program IDs used in owner and configuration validation.
Defines the canonical GlobalConfig layout and world fields that setup reads before packing worldId and GlobalConfig into PlayerSession.
Resolves the runtime Solana cluster and endpoint configuration used by the production chain client and retained Devnet capture.
Exercises wallet logout cleanup behavior, including removal of scoped runtime caches while preserving unrelated browser storage.
Checks PlayerSession PDA derivation, serialized mask and expiry, account ordering, and session-aware Chunk mining instruction behavior.
Coordinates local placement prediction, chain-adapter submission, confirmation, and rollback around the currently disabled chain placement result.
Coordinates the player-facing mining action that can eventually call one of the session-aware Chunk consumer paths.
Synchronizes the owner-backed Backpack visible to Play, whose dense records are changed by the audited Backpack removal handlers.
Exercises production adapter normalization for submitted and skipped chain-action results, including placement delegation.
